payload.professional_persona: Jaten Sharma, a 49‑year‑old institutional cleaning specialist, they apply their methodical planning and warm teamwork to ensure spotless floors, smooth laundry cycles, and timely minor repairs while mentoring junior staff toward reliable service.
payload.linguistic_persona: Jaten Sharma grew up speaking Assamese and can read and write basic Assamese script, they manage modest spoken Hindi for religious and community interactions, and they encounter English only through occasional workplace terms.
payload.sports_persona: Jaten Sharma follows cricket passionately, they cheer for the Indian national team and root for the Kolkata Knight Riders in IPL, while also playing weekly volleyball matches in the tea garden courtyard and taking early morning jogs along the Brahmaputra banks to stay fit.
payload.arts_persona: Jaten Sharma enjoys Assamese folk melodies by Zubeen Garg and Papon, they participate in Bihu dance gatherings, and relax by watching classic Bollywood films starring Shah Rukh Khan and Hindi music from Arijit Singh.
payload.travel_persona: Jaten Sharma prefers organized bus tours, they have explored nearby tea estates in Darjeeling and Shillong, and dream of a pilgrimage to Varanasi and a relaxing stay in Kerala's backwaters, while balancing travel costs with their family's financial plans.
payload.culinary_persona: Jaten Sharma enjoys traditional Assamese fare such as masor tenga (sour fish curry), akhuni (spicy pork relish), and steaming pitha, they prefer simple home-cooked meals with mustard seeds and bamboo shoots, but rarely cook themselves, instead relishing community feasts and a steaming cup of locally grown tea.
payload.persona: Jaten Sharma is a meticulous housekeeping pro whose heart beats to Bihu drums, they sneak a late‑night chai after finishing their rounds, and they dream of launching their own cleaning venture post‑retirement.
payload.cultural_background: Jaten hails from the Assamese cultural milieu of rural Dibrugarh, where tea estates and agricultural life shape daily rhythms. He is raised in a community that values simple hospitality, collective celebrations, and harmonious coexistence. The annual Bihu festivals, folk music, and traditional dance are central to his identity, as is a deep respect for elder family members and neighbours.
payload.linguistic_background: His mother tongue is Assamese (Asamiya). With education limited to below primary level, his literacy is confined to basic Assamese script for everyday tasks. He can comprehend spoken Hindi to a modest extent, especially religious and community contexts, but cannot read or write it fluently. Exposure to English is minimal and largely restricted to occasional workplace terminology.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Jaten is proficient in institutional cleaning and sanitation, adept at floor polishing, laundry handling, and managing cleaning inventories. He possesses practical knowledge of basic maintenance tasks such as minor plumbing and electrical repairs, ensures safety compliance, and coordinates effectively with team members to meet scheduled cleaning routines.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Institutional cleaning","Floor polishing","Laundry handling","Inventory management","Basic maintenance","Time management","Team coordination","Safety compliance","Simple bookkeeping"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: He enjoys participating in Bihu dances, listening to Assamese folk music, watching cricket matches, gardening, cooking traditional Assamese dishes, volunteering in community events, strolling through tea gardens, and reading devotional literature.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Bihu dance","Assamese folk music","Cricket watching","Gardening","Cooking Assamese dishes","Community volunteering","Walking in tea gardens","Reading devotional literature"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: He aims to progress to a senior housekeeping supervisor role within his institution, mentor junior staff, improve his literacy through adult education classes, and eventually establish a small independent cleaning service after retirement to provide financial security for his family.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 49-year-old male from rural Dibrugarh, Assam, currently married, works as an institutional housekeeper with below primary education. His first language is Assamese, and he has limited Hindi comprehension. Clinically, he may have limited health literacy and relies on community support for daily living. His lifestyle includes a sedentary job with occasional physical activity from cleaning tasks.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]

  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]

  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Automated SMS Notifications for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Assamese (code=as, script=Bengali). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Assam, district=Dibrugarh.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Bengali script for Assamese (not English; not a different Indic script).

LENGTH HARD LIMIT: entire SMS ≤ ~500 characters, but you MUST still include every mandatory entity tag below at least once (compact tags OK).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 49
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: House Keeper (Institutional)
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Assam
- district: Dibrugarh
- first_language: Assamese
- clinical_persona_summary: A 49-year-old male from rural Dibrugarh, Assam, currently married, works as an institutional housekeeper with below primary education. His first language is Assamese, and he has limited Hindi comprehension. Clinically, he may have limited health literacy and relies on community support for daily living. His lifestyle includes a sedentary job with occasional physical activity from cleaning tasks.

Document format guide for Automated SMS Notifications (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
CRITICAL: keep the WHOLE SMS under ~400 characters / 2–4 sentences. Not a form dump.
Example:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text: [[PATIENT_NAME|Ranjan Das]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-02]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Dibrugarh PHC]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. B. Borah]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-002]].

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Bengali (code=bn, script=Bengali). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=West Bengal, district=Barddhaman.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Bengali script for Bengali (not English; not a different Indic script).

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 27
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: West Bengal
- district: Barddhaman
- first_language: Bengali
- clinical_persona_summary: A 27-year-old female from West Bengal, currently married, with primary education and a rural residence in Barddhaman. Her first language is Bengali, and she is a homemaker. Clinically, she has a history of traditional dietary habits, including regular consumption of sweets and rice-based meals.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton — put demographics + device/network IDs ONLY in a short
session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|...]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
--- chat ---
Patient: <symptom turn — no AGE/GENDER/PHONE re-tags>
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]: <advice turn>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
- URL — URL (Personal Website/Profile) e.g. [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
- IMEI_NUMBER — IMEI Number e.g. [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] [[AGE|27]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-WB-2024-0815-01]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.wbhealth.in/visit/APT-WB-2024-0815-01]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|anjali.mondal.wb@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Barddhaman District Hospital]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Namaste doctor. Ami Anjali, 27 bochorer. Ami 3 masher pregnant. Aajkal pete beshi byatha hochhe, especially right side e. Eta ki normal?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sujata Chatterjee]]: Namaste Anjali. Ami Sujata Chatterjee, Barddhaman District Hospital theke bolchhi. Apnar symptoms ta shunte khub bhalo laglo na. Right side pete byatha hoite pare pregnancy te, kintu amra ekbar check kore nei. Apni ki kono bleeding ba spotting dekhchhen?
Patient: Na, bleeding nei. Shudhu byatha. Ami khub beshi chinta korchhi. Ami ki kichu korte pari?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sujata Chatterjee]]: Apni chinta na korun. Eta normal hote pare, kintu amra ekta ultrasound kore dekhte hobe. Apni ki apnar last menstrual period er date ta mone achhe? Apni ki apnar doctor er sathe kotha bolechhen?
Patient: Haan, ami ekbar dekhiyechhi. Doctor bolechhilen 3 masher. Ami 15th August er por theke byatha ta beshi hochhe. Ami ki kichu khabar khete pari?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sujata Chatterjee]]: 15th August er por theke byatha ta beshi hoite pare. Apni khub beshi chinta na korun. Apni eto beshi rice khete na. Apni beshi kore shak, shaksobji, protein, ebong fresh fruits khete paren. Apni beshi kore paani khaben. Apni rest korun. Ami apnar jonno ekta appointment book korchhi. Apni apnar MRN [[MRN|MRN-WB-2024-0815-001]] ta niye asben. Apni ki apnar husband er phone number ta dite paren?
Patient: Haan, amar husband er number 9876543210. Apni ki kichu medicine diben?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sujata Chatterjee]]: Apni rest korun. Ami apnar jonno ekta prescription pathachhi. Apni apnar local pharmacy theke medicine ta niye asben. Apni apnar husband er sathe kotha bolben. Apni apnar family ke bolben. Apni amake update diben. Apni bhalo thakben.
Patient: Dhonnobad doctor. Apni onek help korechhen.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sujata Chatterjee]]: Apni bhalo thakun. Apni amake bolben.

payload.professional_persona: Anoara Das, a 27‑year‑old village‑based home caterer‑to‑be, blends their knack for meticulous budgeting, authentic Bengali sweets preparation, and natural flair for community event coordination into a practical, socially‑driven small‑business vision.
payload.linguistic_persona: Anoara Das, fluent in Bengali, can read and write simple household notes, understands conversational Hindi, and recognizes everyday English words on signs and ads, making them a functional multilingual communicator for village life.
payload.sports_persona: Anoara Das, a passionate weekend kabaddi enthusiast who trains with the local village team and fervently supports the Kolkata Knight Riders during IPL, combines regular matches with a disciplined yoga practice, showcasing their love for teamwork, physical fitness, and community spirit.
payload.arts_persona: Anoara Das, a devoted admirer of Baul folk music who often listens to Paban Das Baul while stitching Kantha quilts and delights in Rabindranath Tagore’s lyrical songs, brings cultural storytelling into everyday conversation, illustrating their deep‑rooted artistic sensibility.
payload.travel_persona: Anoara Das, who primarily explores nearby Kolkata and the Sundarbans during weekend getaways, occasionally saves for a pilgrimage to Varanasi, and prefers planning trips through word‑of‑mouth recommendations from fellow villagers, reflecting their modest yet community‑driven travel style.
payload.culinary_persona: Anoara Das, a skilled home cook who excels at preparing rosogolla, sandesh, mishti doi, and fragrant panta bhat with mustard greens, enjoys sourcing fresh cumin and jaggery from the local market, and treats each family gathering as a culinary showcase, yet sometimes indulges in extra sweets, revealing their sweet‑tooth quirk.
payload.persona: Anoara Das, a sociable, methodical 27‑year‑old from a farming village, balances their love for traditional sweets, community storytelling, and pragmatic financial planning with a curious yet practical mindset.
payload.cultural_background: Born and raised in the agrarian heartland of Barddhaman, West Bengal, Anoara Das grew up immersed in the rich tapestry of Bengali culture. Her daily life follows the rhythms of monsoon‑dependent farming, the colourful celebrations of Durga Puja and Poila Boishakh, and the warm, community‑centric spirit of rural villages where neighbours gather for adda and share homemade sweets like rosogolla. Hindu traditions interweave with folk music, storytelling and a strong sense of hospitality. While she embraces modern ideas, she remains rooted in cultural values of respect for elders, collective harmony, and the simplicity of village life.
payload.linguistic_background: Anoara’s first language is Bengali, and she is a native speaker with functional literacy acquired through primary schooling. She can read and write simple Bengali texts, such as household notes, recipes, and basic letters. Although her formal education did not include a second language, she understands spoken Hindi from regional exposure and can follow basic Hindi conversations. She has limited proficiency in English, primarily recognizing common words on signage and advertisements.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Anoara’s skill set revolves around efficient household management and community engagement. She excels at preparing authentic Bengali dishes and sweets, budgeting family expenses, and coordinating festive events such as Durga Puja pandals. Her strong interpersonal abilities make her a natural facilitator in village “adda” gatherings, where she often mediates conversations and encourages cooperation. Additionally, she possesses basic literacy in Bengali, practical gardening knowledge, and rudimentary digital skills for using a mobile phone to stay connected with relatives and access information.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Bengali cooking', 'Household budgeting', 'Festive event planning', 'Community coordination', 'Interpersonal communication', 'Basic Bengali literacy', 'Gardening', 'Kantha embroidery', 'Mobile phone digital literacy']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Anoara enjoys lively village ‘adda’ sessions where she engages in storytelling, debates, and sharing of recipes. She is an avid listener of Baul folk music and often practices simple Kantha stitching as a creative outlet. Gardening in her backyard, preparing traditional sweets, and reading devotional tales are regular pastimes. On weekends, she likes to watch local folk dance performances and participate in community sports like kabaddi with friends.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Participating in village adda', 'Listening to Baul music', 'Gardening', 'Cooking Bengali sweets', 'Kantha stitching', 'Reading devotional stories', 'Watching folk dance performances', 'Playing Kabaddi with friends']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Anoara aspires to transform her passion for Bengali cuisine into a modest home‑based catering business, specializing in traditional sweets for festivals and family occasions. She also aims to take a leadership role in her village’s women’s self‑help group, organizing financial literacy workshops and promoting small‑scale entrepreneurship among fellow homemakers. To support these goals, she plans to enrol in an adult literacy program to improve her reading and writing skills and to obtain a short‑term certificate in nutrition and food safety.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 27-year-old female from West Bengal, currently married, with primary education and a rural background. She is a homemaker and native Bengali speaker, likely with functional literacy. Clinically, she may have a diet rich in traditional sweets and carbohydrates, with potential for lifestyle-related metabolic concerns. Her lifestyle is likely sedentary with limited structured exercise, though she may engage in community activities.

Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Barddhaman District Hospital]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-001]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] [[AGE|27]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Suman Chatterjee]]
Procedure / findings: Patient presented with a history of recurrent right lower quadrant pain for the past three months, with the current episode being acute and severe. Examination revealed tenderness and guarding in the right iliac fossa. An ultrasound showed a 6mm calculus in the distal ureter with mild hydronephrosis. The decision was made for a ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy. The procedure was performed under spinal anesthesia. A 6.5Fr semi-rigid ureteroscope was inserted, and the stone was visualized at the ureterovesical junction. Laser lithotripsy was performed using a 200-micron Holmium:YAG laser fiber, and the stone was fragmented into fine dust, which was then removed with a stone basket. The procedure was completed successfully, and a double-J stent was placed. The patient tolerated the procedure well and was shifted to the recovery room in stable condition.
[[BED_NUMBER|12]]
[[MRN|MRN-WB-2024-0815-001]]
[[RELATIVE_NAME|Ranjan Mondal]]
[[PATIENT_ID|PID-WB-2024-0815-001]]

payload.skills_and_expertise: Anoara’s skill set revolves around efficient household management and community engagement. Her strong interpersonal abilities make her a natural facilitator in village “adda” gatherings, where she often mediates conversations and encourages cooperation. রোগ নির্ণয়:
পালমোনারি টিউবারকিউলোসিস, মাল্টিড্রাগ-রেজিস্ট্যান্ট (এম.ডি.আর-টি.বি)

চিকিৎসা পরিকল্পনা:
সংশোধিত জাতীয় যক্ষ্মা নিয়ন্ত্রণ কর্মসূচির (আর.এন.টি.সি.পি) নির্দেশিকা অনুযায়ী এম.ডি.আর-টি.বি রেজিমেন শুরু করা হয়েছে। রোগীকে চিকিৎসার প্রতি আনুগত্য এবং পার্শ্বপ্রতিক্রিয়া সম্পর্কে পরামর্শ দেওয়া হয়েছে। 2 মাস পরে ফলো-আপ কফ পরীক্ষার সময় নির্ধারণ করা হয়েছে।

payload.professional_persona: Sharon Merry, a 28‑year‑old sustainable agriculture specialist, combines meticulous planning, data‑driven farm management and traditional crop wisdom to lead community initiatives and spearhead an organic certification program, while their organized nature ensures every planting schedule stays on track.
payload.linguistic_persona: Sharon Merry is fluent in their native language, proficient in Hindi and English, and conversational in Assamese, employing each for farm coordination, official paperwork and neighborly chats.
payload.sports_persona: Sharon Merry enjoys playing volleyball with the village women’s club, follows NorthEast United FC in the Indian Super League, and practices yoga daily to stay fit, occasionally joining weekend hikes in the hills.
payload.arts_persona: Sharon Merry cherishes traditional folk songs and dances, regularly joining community performances, while also listening to Bollywood artists like Arijit Singh and Shreya Ghoshal, and finds creative relief in knitting colorful scarves.
payload.travel_persona: Sharon Merry prefers modest, planned trips, such as exploring the tea gardens of Assam, the wildlife of Kaziranga, and the misty waterfalls of Meghalaya, while dreaming of a relaxed Kerala backwater cruise when finances allow.
payload.culinary_persona: Sharon Merry enjoys savoring aromatic bamboo shoot curry, tangy fish ‘tenga’, and mustard‑seed pitha, often relishing fresh coriander, green chilies and locally sourced ginger that accompany these dishes, though they seldom cook themselves and prefer to eat meals prepared by family members.
payload.persona: Sharon Merry blends meticulous farm planning with a love for folk rhythms, yet often battles a restless mind that fuels both relentless curiosity and occasional over‑thinking.
payload.cultural_background: Sharon Merry belongs to the Bodo tribal community of Nagaland, a group known for its agrarian way of life and strong communal bonds. Growing up in the rural outskirts of Dimapur, she was immersed in a cultural mix of indigenous Bodo customs, Christian church traditions introduced during the missionary era, and the broader Indian rural milieu. Her family’s farming practices, folk songs, and seasonal festivals reflect a synthesis of tribal heritage and Christian values, creating a balanced identity that respects both traditional rituals and modern aspirations.
payload.linguistic_background: Sharon’s mother tongue is Bodo, in which she reads, writes, and speaks fluently. She acquired Hindi during her school years and is comfortable with both spoken and written Hindi, using it for official communication and everyday interactions. Her postgraduate studies required proficiency in English, which she now uses for academic reading, report writing, and interacting with agricultural extension services. Additionally, she learned Assamese to communicate with neighbours in the adjoining Assam region; her competence is conversational, with basic reading skills. This multilingual repertoire enables her to navigate the diverse linguistic landscape of northeastern India.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Sharon possesses extensive expertise in crop cultivation, including soil health assessment, seed selection, pest management, and sustainable irrigation techniques. Her postgraduate education equips her with knowledge of agronomic principles, farm budgeting, and data‑driven decision making. She is adept at using basic digital tools for record‑keeping and market price tracking. Her multilingual abilities enable effective communication with farm laborers, local traders, and extension officers. Additionally, her organizational skills allow her to plan planting schedules, coordinate community farming initiatives, and maintain compliance with government agricultural schemes.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Crop cultivation", "Soil health assessment", "Seed selection", "Integrated pest management", "Sustainable irrigation", "Agronomic analysis", "Farm budgeting", "Data-driven decision making", "Basic digital record-keeping", "Market price monitoring", "Multilingual communication", "Project planning", "Community farming coordination", "Compliance with agricultural schemes"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Sharon enjoys traditional Bodo folk songs and dance, often participating in community festivals. She likes gardening beyond her farm, growing ornamental plants and herbs for personal use. Reading Christian devotional literature and attending church choir rehearsals provide her spiritual solace. To manage stress she practices yoga and mindfulness meditation. She also follows agricultural webinars and reads journals on sustainable farming, reflecting her curiosity. Occasionally, she knits scarves using locally sourced yarn, blending creativity with practicality.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Traditional Bodo folk songs and dance", "Gardening ornamental plants and herbs", "Reading Christian devotional literature", "Church choir participation", "Yoga and mindfulness meditation", "Following agricultural webinars", "Reading sustainable farming journals", "Knitting scarves with local yarn"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Sharon aims to transform her family farm into a model of sustainable, high‑value crop production by integrating modern agronomic practices with traditional Bodo knowledge. Over the next five years she plans to pursue a certification in organic farming, adopt precision irrigation, and establish a farmer‑producer cooperative that can negotiate better market prices for her community. She also aspires to mentor younger women in her village, promoting agricultural education and entrepreneurship. Ultimately, she envisions a regional agro‑innovation hub where research, training, and market linkages converge, improving livelihoods and reducing rural out‑migration.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 28-year-old female graduate working as a cultivator in rural Dimapur, Nagaland, who is currently married and speaks Bodo and Hindi. She has a graduate education and is a cultivator by occupation.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]

  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete ER Triage & Nursing Notes for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Bodo (code=brx, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Nagaland, district=Dimapur.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Devanagari script for Bodo (not English; not a different Indic script).

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once in the arrival/RTA/ambulance line. Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 28
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Cultivator, Crop
- education_level: Graduate & above
- zone: Rural
- state: Nagaland
- district: Dimapur
- first_language: Bodo
- clinical_persona_summary: A 28-year-old female graduate working as a cultivator in rural Dimapur, Nagaland, who is currently married and speaks Bodo and Hindi. She has a graduate education and is a cultivator by occupation.

Document format guide for ER Triage & Nursing Notes (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|22]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|...]]
Arrived by ambulance / RTA vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|...]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Vitals / acuity: <short notes>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Dimapur District Hospital]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0815-001]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Lalita Horo]] [[AGE|28]] [[GENDER|Female]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|03]]
Arrived by ambulance vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|NL01A1234]] (exactly once).
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|Biren Horo]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Kevi Angami]]

Abdomen is tender in the right lower quadrant with guarding. Initial assessment suggests possible acute appendicitis or an ovarian cyst complication. Patient is alert and oriented. Initial blood work and ultrasound have been ordered. Patient is NPO pending surgical evaluation.

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Bodo (code=brx, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Nagaland, district=Dimapur.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Devanagari script for Bodo (not English; not a different Indic script).

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 28
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Cultivator, Crop
- education_level: Graduate & above
- zone: Rural
- state: Nagaland
- district: Dimapur
- first_language: Bodo
- clinical_persona_summary: A 28-year-old female cultivator from Dimapur, Nagaland, who is married and holds a graduate degree, works in agriculture. She speaks Bodo and Hindi, and resides in a rural setting. Her lifestyle includes traditional farming and community involvement.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton — put demographics + device/network IDs ONLY in a short
session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|...]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
--- chat ---
Patient: <symptom turn — no AGE/GENDER/PHONE re-tags>
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]: <advice turn>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
- URL — URL (Personal Website/Profile) e.g. [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
- IMEI_NUMBER — IMEI Number e.g. [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Rina Boro]] [[AGE|28]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|rina.boro@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Dimapur District Hospital]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Namaskar Doctor Sahab, I am [[PATIENT_NAME|Rina Boro]] from Dimapur. I have been having a persistent cough for the last three weeks, along with some fever in the evenings. It started as a dry cough, but now it is bringing up some phlegm. I also feel very tired and have lost a little weight. I am a cultivator, so I am always outdoors. What could this be?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]: Hello Rina, thank you for reaching out. I understand you are [[AGE|28]] and [[GENDER|Female]]. A cough with fever and weight loss can be due to many reasons. To help me understand better, could you tell me if you have any other symptoms like body aches, headache, or shortness of breath? Also, have you been in contact with anyone who was sick recently?
Patient: I do have some body aches, especially in my back and joints. No headache or shortness of breath. I haven't been in close contact with anyone sick, but I work in the fields all day.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]: Thank you for that information. Given your symptoms of cough, fever, weight loss, and body aches, we should consider a few possibilities. The most common would be a prolonged respiratory infection. However, we must also rule out other conditions. I would like to arrange for a few tests for you. I will send a prescription for a chest X-ray and a complete blood count (CBC) to the lab near your location. Please get these done and share the reports with me. In the meantime, you can take paracetamol for the fever and aches. Please ensure you are drinking plenty of fluids.
Patient: Okay Doctor, I can go to the lab tomorrow. What about the prescription? How will I get it?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]: I have sent the prescription electronically to the registered pharmacy near Dimapur District Hospital. You can collect your medicines from there. Please remember to take the medicines as prescribed. We will review your test results once they are available and plan the next steps. Stay hydrated and get some rest.
Patient: Thank you Doctor. I will get the tests done and send you the reports.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]: You are welcome, Rina. Take care and we will speak again soon.

payload.professional_persona: Sharon Merry, a 28‑year‑old sustainable agriculture specialist, combines meticulous planning, data‑driven farm management and traditional crop wisdom to lead community initiatives and spearhead an organic certification program, while their organized nature ensures every planting schedule stays on track.
payload.linguistic_persona: Sharon Merry is fluent in their native language, proficient in Hindi and English, and conversational in Assamese, employing each for farm coordination, official paperwork and neighborly chats.
payload.sports_persona: Sharon Merry enjoys playing volleyball with the village women’s club, follows NorthEast United FC in the Indian Super League, and practices yoga daily to stay fit, occasionally joining weekend hikes in the hills.
payload.arts_persona: Sharon Merry cherishes traditional folk songs and dances, regularly joining community performances, while also listening to Bollywood artists like Arijit Singh and Shreya Ghoshal, and finds creative relief in knitting colorful scarves.
payload.travel_persona: Sharon Merry prefers modest, planned trips, such as exploring the tea gardens of Assam, the wildlife of Kaziranga, and the misty waterfalls of Meghalaya, while dreaming of a relaxed Kerala backwater cruise when finances allow.
payload.culinary_persona: Sharon Merry enjoys savoring aromatic bamboo shoot curry, tangy fish ‘tenga’, and mustard‑seed pitha, often relishing fresh coriander, green chilies and locally sourced ginger that accompany these dishes, though they seldom cook themselves and prefer to eat meals prepared by family members.
payload.persona: Sharon Merry blends meticulous farm planning with a love for folk rhythms, yet often battles a restless mind that fuels both relentless curiosity and occasional over‑thinking.
payload.cultural_background: Sharon Merry belongs to the Bodo tribal community of Nagaland, a group known for its agrarian way of life and strong communal bonds. Growing up in the rural outskirts of Dimapur, she was immersed in a cultural mix of indigenous Bodo customs, Christian church traditions introduced during the missionary era, and the broader Indian rural milieu. Her family’s farming practices, folk songs, and seasonal festivals reflect a synthesis of tribal heritage and Christian values, creating a balanced identity that respects both traditional rituals and modern aspirations.
payload.linguistic_background: Sharon’s mother tongue is Bodo, in which she reads, writes, and speaks fluently. She acquired Hindi during her school years and is comfortable with both spoken and written Hindi, using it for official communication and everyday interactions. Her postgraduate studies required proficiency in English, which she now uses for academic reading, report writing, and interacting with agricultural extension services. Additionally, she learned Assamese to communicate with neighbours in the adjoining Assam region; her competence is conversational, with basic reading skills. This multilingual repertoire enables her to navigate the diverse linguistic landscape of northeastern India.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Sharon possesses extensive expertise in crop cultivation, including soil health assessment, seed selection, pest management, and sustainable irrigation techniques. Her postgraduate education equips her with knowledge of agronomic principles, farm budgeting, and data‑driven decision making. She is adept at using basic digital tools for record‑keeping and market price tracking. Her multilingual abilities enable effective communication with farm laborers, local traders, and extension officers. Additionally, her organizational skills allow her to plan planting schedules, coordinate community farming initiatives, and maintain compliance with government agricultural schemes.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Crop cultivation", "Soil health assessment", "Seed selection", "Integrated pest management", "Sustainable irrigation", "Agronomic analysis", "Farm budgeting", "Data-driven decision making", "Basic digital record-keeping", "Market price monitoring", "Multilingual communication", "Project planning", "Community farming coordination", "Compliance with agricultural schemes"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Sharon enjoys traditional Bodo folk songs and dance, often participating in community festivals. She likes gardening beyond her farm, growing ornamental plants and herbs for personal use. Reading Christian devotional literature and attending church choir rehearsals provide her spiritual solace. To manage stress she practices yoga and mindfulness meditation. She also follows agricultural webinars and reads journals on sustainable farming, reflecting her curiosity. Occasionally, she knits scarves using locally sourced yarn, blending creativity with practicality.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Traditional Bodo folk songs and dance", "Gardening ornamental plants and herbs", "Reading Christian devotional literature", "Church choir participation", "Yoga and mindfulness meditation", "Following agricultural webinars", "Reading sustainable farming journals", "Knitting scarves with local yarn"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Sharon aims to transform her family farm into a model of sustainable, high‑value crop production by integrating modern agronomic practices with traditional Bodo knowledge. Over the next five years she plans to pursue a certification in organic farming, adopt precision irrigation, and establish a farmer‑producer cooperative that can negotiate better market prices for her community. She also aspires to mentor younger women in her village, promoting agricultural education and entrepreneurship. Ultimately, she envisions a regional agro‑innovation hub where research, training, and market linkages converge, improving livelihoods and reducing rural out‑migration.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 28-year-old female cultivator from Dimapur, Nagaland, who is married and holds a graduate degree, speaks Bodo and Hindi. She works in agriculture and resides in a rural area, with lifestyle cues indicating a physically active, community-oriented lifestyle. Her background suggests a strong connection to traditional practices and a preference for locally sourced, agrarian living.

payload.generated_text:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Dimapur]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Hema Boro]] [[AGE|28]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. K. Jamir]]
Procedure / findings: Patient is a 28-year-old female cultivator from Dimapur, Nagaland, admitted for an emergency cesarean section at 38 weeks gestation for fetal distress. The patient is married and has one previous normal vaginal delivery. On examination, her vitals were stable with a blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg and heart rate of 88 bpm. Fetal monitoring showed non-reassuring heart rate patterns. An emergency decision was made for a lower segment cesarean section. The procedure was performed under spinal anesthesia. A midline vertical skin incision was made, followed by a transverse uterine incision. A live female infant weighing 2.8 kg was delivered with Apgar scores of 7 at 1 minute and 9 at 5 minutes. The placenta was delivered spontaneously. Hemostasis was achieved, and the uterine incision was closed in two layers. The abdominal wall was closed in layers. The patient tolerated the procedure well. Post-operative vitals are stable. The infant is stable and with the mother. [[BED_NUMBER|12]] [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]] [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sanjay Boro]]

payload.arts_persona: Raghveer Singh enjoys listening to Punjabi folk and Bhangra tracks by Gurdas Maan and Diljit Dosanjh, which lift their spirits after work, and regularly reads retail‑management columns by Pradeep Sinha in Hindi magazines, occasionally attending village cultural evenings featuring folk dance performances.
payload.travel_persona: Raghveer Singh prefers short, budget‑friendly weekend trips, having visited Amritsar’s Golden Temple and the hill station of Shimla by train, while planning future trips to the historic markets of old Delhi and the coastal town of Goa as finances allow.
payload.culinary_persona: Raghveer Singh enjoys tasting and occasionally experimenting with classic Punjabi dishes such as sarson da saag with makki di roti, chole bhature, and butter chicken, preferring fresh mustard leaves, maize flour, garam masala, and a tangy mango pickle; they also relish the crunch of golgappa from local street‑food stalls.
payload.persona: Raghveer Singh blends disciplined retail expertise with a love for Bhangra beats, a habit of organizing every shelf like a ledger, and an unflappable calm that makes them the village’s trusted problem‑solver.
payload.cultural_background: Raghveer Singh belongs to a Sikh family settled in the rural outskirts of Jalandhar, Punjab. Though his mother tongue is Dogri, a language primarily spoken in the Jammu region, his family has lived in the fertile plains of Punjab for generations, absorbing Punjabi customs while preserving their Dogri heritage. The village community is tightly knit, with daily life revolving around the local Gurdwara, agricultural cycles, and seasonal fairs such as Baisakhi. Growing up, he participated in traditional folk music, communal langar preparations, and seasonal harvest festivals, which reinforced a sense of collective responsibility and humility. Punjabi, being the lingua franca of Jalandhar, is his third language; he uses it daily at the shop for customer interactions and can understand spoken Punjabi fluently, while his reading ability in Gurmukhi is functional for signage and basic labels. payload.skills_and_expertise: Raghveer's core expertise lies in retail operations. He excels at systematic inventory control, ensuring stock levels are accurately tracked using simple ledger methods. His proficiency in cash handling and point‑of‑sale transactions minimizes errors. Multilingual communication—fluent in Dogri, Hindi, and conversational Punjabi—allows him to serve a diverse clientele with ease. He is skilled at merchandising, arranging product displays in a tidy, visually appealing manner. His disciplined work ethic translates into consistent store hygiene and punctuality. He also possesses basic bookkeeping, using Excel spreadsheets for monthly expense summaries, and has a curiosity for digital payment systems, enabling him to adopt new technologies such as UPI and QR‑code payments with minimal disruption. His calm demeanor helps resolve customer issues calmly and efficiently.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]

  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]

  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Discharge Summary for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Dogri (code=doi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Punjab, district=Jalandhar.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Devanagari script for Dogri (not English; not a different Indic script).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 34
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Shop Attendant
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Rural
- state: Punjab
- district: Jalandhar
- first_language: Dogri
- clinical_persona_summary: Male, 34, currently married, shop attendant with higher secondary education, living in rural Jalandhar, Punjab, and speaking Dogri. He maintains a disciplined lifestyle with regular physical activity and consumes a diet rich in traditional Punjabi staples. His cultural background is Sikh, and he is fluent in Dogri and Hindi.

Document format guide for Discharge Summary (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] DOB [[DOB|1994-06-21]] [[AGE|30]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]] | Course / advice: <concise paragraphs matching domain + persona sex/age>

MANDATORY inline entities (14 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- ABHA_ADDRESS — ABHA Address e.g. [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
- CASTE — Caste e.g. [[CASTE|OBC]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Civil Hospital Jalandhar]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Baldev Singh]] DOB [[DOB|1990-02-15]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Male]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0718]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|A1]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|05]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]] | Course / advice: The patient was admitted with a chief complaint of high-grade fever, productive cough, and shortness of breath for three days. On examination, he was febrile with a temperature of 102.4°F, heart rate 110 bpm, respiratory rate 24 breaths/min, and blood pressure 130/85 mmHg. Chest auscultation revealed bilateral crackles. Investigations showed leukocytosis with neutrophilia and elevated C-reactive protein. A chest X-ray demonstrated consolidation in the right lower lobe. The patient was diagnosed with community-acquired pneumonia and treated with intravenous ceftriaxone and azithromycin. He responded well to treatment, with fever subsiding by the second day. The patient was afebrile, hemodynamically stable, and clinically improving on the third day of admission. He was discharged on oral amoxicillin-clavulanate for a total of seven days of antibiotic therapy. He was advised to complete the full course of medication, maintain adequate hydration, and follow up with his primary care physician in one week. He was counselled on the importance of vaccination against pneumococcal disease and influenza. The patient was advised to return immediately if he experiences recurrence of fever, increased dyspnea, or chest pain. He was discharged in a stable condition with instructions to continue his routine activities and maintain a nutritious diet. The patient's wife, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Jaspreet Kaur]], was present and educated about the discharge plan. The patient's [[ABHA_ADDRESS|baldev.singh@abdm]] and [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] were verified for follow-up. The patient belongs to the [[CASTE|SC]] community and resides in [[VILLAGE|Bhikhiwind]]. His [[DISTRICT|Jalandhar]] address is confirmed.
