payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Referral Letter for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Dogri (code=doi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Punjab, district=Jalandhar.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Devanagari script for Dogri (not English; not a different Indic script).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 34
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Shop Attendant
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Rural
- state: Punjab
- district: Jalandhar
- first_language: Dogri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 34-year-old male from Jalandhar, Punjab, currently married, working as a shop attendant with higher secondary education, and fluent in Dogri and Hindi. He resides in a rural zone and follows a disciplined lifestyle with a calm demeanor. His cultural background is Sikh, and he enjoys Punjabi folk music and traditional Punjabi cuisine.

Document format guide for Referral Letter (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-01]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|36]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Reason: <domain-appropriate clinical reason matching persona>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- REFERRAL_ID — Referral ID e.g. [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PASSPORT_NUMBER — Passport Number e.g. [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
- DRIVING_LICENCE — Driving Licence Number e.g. [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-0815]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Civil Hospital Jalandhar]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Gurpreet Singh]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Balwinder Singh]], [[AGE|34]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|Jalandhar]]
Reason: Persistent cough with constitutional symptoms for evaluation and management of suspected pulmonary tuberculosis with comorbid type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Dear Sir/Madam,

This is to refer our patient, [[PATIENT_NAME|Balwinder Singh]], a 34-year-old male shop attendant from a rural area of Jalandhar district, for further evaluation and treatment. The patient presents with a two-month history of productive cough, low-grade fever, night sweats, and weight loss. He is a known case of type 2 diabetes mellitus, poorly controlled on oral hypoglycemic agents. His [[MRN|MRN-2024-0712-004]] records show an HbA1c of 9.2%.

On examination, he is afebrile with a pulse of 88/min and blood pressure of 128/82 mmHg. Respiratory examination reveals coarse crepitations in the right upper zone. His vitals are stable. Given the high clinical suspicion for pulmonary tuberculosis and the presence of diabetes, which complicates treatment, we request your expert opinion for confirmation of diagnosis and initiation of anti-tubercular therapy under the Revised National Tuberculosis Control Programme (RNTCP).

For any further clarification, you may contact us at [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]. The patient's father, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Jaswinder Singh]], will accompany him and can be reached at the same number. We have attached his [[DRIVING_LICENCE|PB04 201001 12345]] and [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|A12345678]] for identification purposes.

We look forward to your expert care.

Sincerely,
Dr. Gurpreet Singh
[[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Gurpreet Singh]]

payload.professional_persona: Raghveer Singh is a retail store attendant known for meticulous inventory control, multilingual customer service, and a disciplined, calm approach that blends curiosity for digital payment tools with proven ledger methods; they consistently ensure stock accuracy and streamline checkout processes.
payload.linguistic_persona: Raghveer Singh speaks Dogri fluently, reads and writes it in Devanagari, commands Hindi for all official tasks, converses comfortably in Punjabi and handles basic English for signage, allowing them to seamlessly serve a diverse clientele.
payload.sports_persona: Raghveer Singh actively plays for the local Kabaddi team ‘Shera Kabaddi Club’, trains thrice weekly at the village ground, follows a simple home‑based cardio routine that includes jogging around the fields, and avidly supports the Punjab Warriors in the Pro Kabaddi League.
payload.arts_persona: Raghveer Singh enjoys listening to Punjabi folk and Bhangra tracks by Gurdas Maan and Diljit Dosanjh, which lift their spirits after work, and regularly reads retail‑management columns by Pradeep Sinha in Hindi magazines, occasionally attending village cultural evenings featuring folk dance performances.
payload.travel_persona: Raghveer Singh prefers short, budget‑friendly weekend trips, having visited Amritsar’s Golden Temple and the hill station of Shimla by train, while planning future trips to the historic markets of old Delhi and the coastal town of Goa as finances allow.
payload.culinary_persona: Raghveer Singh enjoys tasting and occasionally experimenting with classic Punjabi dishes such as sarson da saag with makki di roti, chole bhature, and butter chicken, preferring fresh mustard leaves, maize flour, garam masala, and a tangy mango pickle; they also relish the crunch of golgappa from local street‑food stalls.
payload.persona: Raghveer Singh blends disciplined retail expertise with a love for Bhangra beats, a habit of organizing every shelf like a ledger, and an unflappable calm that makes them the village’s trusted problem‑solver.
payload.cultural_background: Raghveer Singh belongs to a Sikh family settled in the rural outskirts of Jalandhar, Punjab. Though his mother tongue is Dogri, a language primarily spoken in the Jammu region, his family has lived in the fertile plains of Punjab for generations, absorbing Punjabi customs while preserving their Dogri heritage. The village community is tightly knit, with daily life revolving around the local Gurdwara, agricultural cycles, and seasonal fairs such as Baisakhi. Growing up, he participated in traditional folk music, communal langar preparations, and seasonal harvest festivals, which reinforced a sense of collective responsibility and humility. His cultural identity blends the disciplined ethos of Sikhism, the practical mindset of a farming hinterland, and the curiosity nurtured by a multilingual environment.
payload.linguistic_background: Raghveer grew up speaking Dogri at home, and is fully fluent in speaking, reading and writing it using the Devanagari script, as taught in his school. His formal education up to higher secondary gave him solid literacy in Hindi; he can comfortably read newspapers, fill forms and maintain ledgers in Hindi. Punjabi, being the lingua franca of Jalandhar, is his third language; he uses it daily at the shop for customer interactions and can understand spoken Punjabi fluently, while his reading ability in Gurmukhi is functional for signage and basic labels. He has basic conversational English, sufficient to understand retail signage and occasional digital payment prompts.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Raghveer's core expertise lies in retail operations. He excels at systematic inventory control, ensuring stock levels are accurately tracked using simple ledger methods. His proficiency in cash handling and point‑of‑sale transactions minimizes errors. Multilingual communication—fluent in Dogri, Hindi, and conversational Punjabi—allows him to serve a diverse clientele with ease. He is skilled at merchandising, arranging product displays in a tidy, visually appealing manner. His disciplined work ethic translates into consistent store hygiene and punctuality. He also possesses basic bookkeeping, using Excel spreadsheets for monthly expense summaries, and has a curiosity for digital payment systems, enabling him to adopt new technologies such as UPI and QR‑code payments with minimal disruption. His calm demeanor helps resolve customer issues calmly and efficiently.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Inventory management', 'Cash handling', 'POS transaction processing', 'Multilingual communication (Dogri, Hindi, Punjabi)', 'Merchandising and visual display', 'Store hygiene and organization', 'Basic bookkeeping (Excel)', 'Digital payment systems (UPI, QR code)', 'Customer service', 'Problem resolution']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Raghveer enjoys listening to Punjabi folk and Bhangra music during his free time, which energizes him after a busy shift. He actively participates in local Kabaddi matches, relishing the sport’s teamwork and physical challenge. Gardening in his small backyard, especially growing seasonal vegetables, provides a calming outlet. He likes experimenting with traditional Punjabi dishes, combining family recipes with his own twists. His curiosity drives him to read articles and books on retail management and digital commerce, often in Hindi magazines. Additionally, he dedicates weekends to volunteering for langar preparation at the Gurdwara, finding fulfillment in seva.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Punjabi folk and Bhangra music', 'Playing Kabaddi (local matches)', 'Gardening (vegetable cultivation)', 'Cooking traditional Punjabi dishes', 'Reading retail management and digital commerce articles (Hindi magazines)', 'Volunteering for Gurdwara langar preparation', 'Exploring new technology (digital payments)']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Raghveer aspires to move from a shop attendant to a store manager within the next three years, taking on responsibilities for inventory planning, staff supervision, and financial oversight. He envisions eventually opening his own neighbourhood grocery outlet, leveraging his deep knowledge of the local market and multilingual customer base. To achieve this, he plans to enroll in short-term retail management and basic accounting courses, and to master digital commerce tools such as UPI integration, inventory software, and data analytics. His curiosity drives him to explore innovative 'jugaad' solutions that streamline operations, while his disciplined approach ensures he meets milestones on schedule. Ultimately, he aims to provide stable employment for his family, contribute to the local economy, and be recognized as a reliable business leader in his community.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Male, 34, currently married, shop attendant, higher secondary education, rural resident in Jalandhar, Punjab, Dogri-speaking. Clinically, he presents with disciplined habits and a calm demeanor, likely reflecting his retail role and community involvement. His lifestyle includes regular physical activity through Kabaddi and jogging, and a diet rich in traditional Punjabi staples.

payload.cultural_background: Shobha Toravi is a Hindu woman from the urban centre of Dakshina Kannada, Karnataka, a coastal region known for its blend of Kannada, Tulu and Konkani cultures. Her second language is Hindi, which she speaks comfortably to a wider Indian clientele and reads simple Hindi scripts such as market signs and devotional pamphlets. She has limited reading and writing proficiency in both languages due to her schooling level, but compensates with strong visual and practical skills.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Shobha is skilled in tailoring and dressmaking, with a keen eye for intricate embroidery and fabric selection. Her work includes client consultation, colour coordination, and adapting traditional motifs into contemporary designs. payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Referral Letter for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into English (code=en, script=Latin). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Karnataka, district=Dakshina Kannada.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Latin script for English (not English; not a different Indic script).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 45
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Tailors and Dress Makers, Other
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Karnataka
- district: Dakshina Kannada
- first_language: English
- clinical_persona_summary: A 45-year-old female tailor from Dakshina Kannada, Karnataka, currently married, with below-primary education, works in an urban setting. Her first language is English, and she manages a modest shop while balancing daily operations with creative design work. Clinically, she presents with anxiety-related symptoms and reports limited formal schooling but functional literacy in English.

Document format guide for Referral Letter (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-01]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|36]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Reason: <domain-appropriate clinical reason matching persona>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- REFERRAL_ID — Referral ID e.g. [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PASSPORT_NUMBER — Passport Number e.g. [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
- DRIVING_LICENCE — Driving Licence Number e.g. [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-0815]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Kasturba Medical College Hospital]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Rao]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Shanthi]], [[AGE|45]] / [[GENDER|Female]], District [[DISTRICT|Dakshina Kannada]]
Reason: Evaluation of persistent anxiety and somatic symptoms with consideration for psychiatric referral.

Dear Sir/Madam,

This is to refer our patient, [[PATIENT_NAME|Shanthi]], a 45-year-old female tailor from Mangaluru, [[DISTRICT|Dakshina Kannada]], [[STATE|Karnataka]], for further evaluation. Her [[MRN|MRN-2024-0721-004]] is on record with us.

The patient presents with a three-month history of increasing anxiety, palpitations, and difficulty sleeping. She reports feeling constantly worried about her family's financial stability and the performance of her tailoring business. These symptoms are accompanied by episodes of dizziness and mild chest discomfort, which have been investigated with an ECG and basic blood work, all of which were within normal limits.

The patient's [[RELATIVE_NAME|husband]], Mr. Prakash, accompanies her and confirms the reported symptoms. He mentions that her anxiety has worsened recently, impacting her ability to manage her daily work and household responsibilities.

For further management, we request a consultation with a psychiatrist to assess for generalized anxiety disorder and to discuss potential treatment options, including counseling and medication if deemed necessary.

Please find the patient's [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] and [[DRIVING_LICENCE|KA01 20100012345]] for your records. Her [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P1234567]] is also available if required.

Thank you for your attention to this case.

Sincerely,
Dr. Anjali Rao
General Medicine
Kasturba Medical College Hospital
[[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-KA-001]]

Radiology report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|KMC Manipal Hospital]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|Shanthi Poojary]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Nair]]
Findings: Ultrasound examination of the abdomen was performed on [[PATIENT_NAME|Shanthi Poojary]], a 45-year-old female. The study was conducted to evaluate the uterus and adnexa. The uterus is anteverted and anteflexed, measuring 8.2 x 5.5 x 4.0 cm. The endometrial cavity is heterogeneous with a thickened posterior layer measuring 8 mm. No focal myometrial lesions are identified. The right and left ovaries are visualized separately, measuring 2.8 x 1.6 x 1.4 cm and 2.5 x 1.5 x 1.3 cm respectively. No ovarian masses or cysts are seen. Color Doppler flow is present within the endometrium. The adnexae are unremarkable. The gallbladder is visualized and shows no evidence of gallstones or wall thickening. The liver and spleen appear normal in echotexture. No free fluid is noted in the peritoneal cavity. Impression: Heterogeneous endometrium with posterior layer thickening, likely related to hormonal status. No evidence of ovarian pathology. No gallbladder pathology. No free fluid. Report completed at 11:30 AM.

payload.professional_persona: Rababen Koli is a budding home‑based entrepreneur focusing on Gujarati culinary crafts, blending meticulous kitchen management with creative folk‑art flair, while their curiosity and organized nature keep innovations grounded in practical jugaad.

payload.sports_persona: Rababen Koli follows cricket passionately, cheering for the Gujarat Titans in the IPL and the Saurashtra team in domestic matches, regularly joins the local women's badminton club for evening games, and occasionally enjoys back‑yard kho‑kho with neighbours during festivals.
payload.arts_persona: Rababen Koli delights in Gujarati folk songs by Hemant Chauhan and Kinjal Dave, sways to Bollywood classics featuring Madhuri Dixit, creates vibrant rangoli designs inspired by traditional motifs, and practices sketching scenes of village life with a humble set of coloured pencils.
payload.travel_persona: Rababen Koli prefers short trips within Gujarat, often visiting the white salt desert of the Rann of Kutch during the full‑moon festival, the historic Somnath temple, and the bustling markets of Ahmedabad for craft supplies, while dreaming of a weekend getaway to Goa's beaches when the family budget allows.
payload.culinary_persona: Rababen Koli loves cooking traditional Gujarati fare, especially dhokla, khandvi, undhiyu, and her tangy homemade mango pickle, seasoning with hing, mustard seeds and fresh coriander, and she savors the crunch of jalebi and dhokla as snack treats.
payload.persona: Rababen Koli is a creatively curious homemaker who blends meticulous household management with folk‑art flair, juggling budget worries and a steady stream of community duties.
payload.cultural_background: Rababen belongs to the Koli community of rural Bhavnagar in Gujarat. The Kolis are traditionally linked to fishing and farming and are celebrated for their vibrant folk traditions, especially Garba and Dandiya dance during Navratri and communal gatherings at the local mandir. Village life follows seasonal agricultural cycles, festivals, and close‑knit family networks. The community values hospitality, collective harmony, and practical ingenuity (jugaad) in daily chores. Growing up surrounded by Gujarati customs, regional cuisine, and oral storytelling of Koli folk songs, Rababen’s curiosity drives her to explore new folk art motifs and experiment with local craft techniques, while she keeps a disciplined routine for household responsibilities.
payload.linguistic_background: Rababen's mother tongue is Gujarati, and she is fully literate in the Gujarati script, having completed primary schooling. She can read and write basic Gujarati texts, especially domestic notes, recipes, and simple letters. Her formal education did not include structured Hindi or English instruction, but she has picked up conversational Hindi from television and community interactions and can understand simple Hindi prayers. Her English exposure is limited to occasional use of English words in daily conversation (e.g., lakhs, crore) and basic reading of labels, but she does not read or write English fluently.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Rababen is proficient in managing a rural household, including meal planning, preparing traditional Gujarati dishes such as dhokla, thali, and farsan, and maintaining kitchen hygiene. She has practical skills in sewing, mirror work embroidery, and creating simple handicrafts like bamboo baskets and decorative diyas. Her organizational ability helps her keep household accounts, budgeting for festivals, and coordinating community events like village fairs and Navratri gatherings. She also possesses basic knowledge of herbal remedies used in the household and can assist neighbours with modest medical advice. Her curiosity about art has led her to experiment with folk painting motifs (Rangoli designs) and basic sketching.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household management", "Traditional Gujarati cooking", "Sewing and mirror work embroidery", "Bamboo basket weaving", "Basic herbal remedy knowledge", "Community event coordination", "Budgeting and financial planning", "Folk painting (Rangoli designs)", "Basic sketching"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Rababen enjoys listening to Gujarati folk music and participating in Garba during Navratri. She loves experimenting with new recipes and decorating dishes with creative garnishes. In her leisure, she paints simple rangoli patterns, practices basic sketching, and weaves bamboo items. Gardening, especially growing seasonal vegetables for her kitchen, provides her with calm. She also follows local news, reads devotional books in Gujarati, and occasionally watches Hindi films to unwind. Her curiosity leads her to explore online videos about craft techniques whenever she finds time.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Gujarati folk music", "Participating in Garba and Dandiya", "Experimenting with new recipes", "Creating rangoli designs", "Basic sketching", "Bamboo weaving", "Gardening seasonal vegetables", "Reading devotional books", "Watching Hindi films", "Exploring online craft tutorials"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Rababen wishes to deepen her expertise in Gujarati culinary arts and traditional crafts, with the ambition to launch a modest home‑based business selling homemade pickles, farsan, and embroidered items. She aims to enrol in government‑run skill development programs for rural women to learn advanced embroidery and small‑scale food processing. Long‑term, she hopes to become financially independent enough to support her parents, fund her younger siblings' education, and contribute to community development projects. While she values her role as a homemaker, she also aspires to nurture her curiosity about art by attending local workshops and possibly mentoring other village girls.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Rababen Koli is a 28-year-old female who is never married, living in rural Bhavnagar, Gujarat, with primary education. She speaks Gujarati as her first language and is a homemaker. She is a retired individual from the Koli community.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.

- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).

payload.travel_persona: Rababen Koli prefers short trips within Gujarat, often visiting the white salt desert of the Rann of Kutch during the full‑moon festival, the historic Somnath temple, and the bustling markets of Ahmedabad for craft supplies, while dreaming of a weekend getaway to Goa's beaches when the family budget allows.
payload.culinary_persona: Rababen Koli loves cooking traditional Gujarati fare, especially dhokla, khandvi, undhiyu, and her tangy homemade mango pickle, seasoning with hing, mustard seeds and fresh coriander, and she savors the crunch of jalebi and dhokla as snack treats.
payload.persona: Rababen Koli is a creatively curious homemaker who blends meticulous household management with folk‑art flair, juggling budget worries and a steady stream of community duties.
payload.cultural_background: Rababen belongs to the Koli community of rural Bhavnagar in Gujarat. The Kolis are traditionally linked to fishing and farming and are celebrated for their vibrant folk traditions, especially Garba and Dandiya dance during Navratri and communal gatherings at the local mandir. Village life follows seasonal agricultural cycles, festivals, and close‑knit family networks. The community values hospitality, collective harmony, and practical ingenuity (jugaad) in daily chores. Growing up surrounded by Gujarati customs, regional cuisine, and oral storytelling of Koli folk songs, Rababen’s curiosity drives her to explore new folk art motifs and experiment with local craft techniques, while she keeps a disciplined routine for household responsibilities.
payload.linguistic_background: Rababen's mother tongue is Gujarati, and she is fully literate in the Gujarati script, having completed primary schooling. She can read and write basic Gujarati texts, especially domestic notes, recipes, and simple letters. Her formal education did not include structured Hindi or English instruction, but she has picked up conversational Hindi from television and community interactions and can understand simple Hindi prayers. Her English exposure is limited to occasional use of English words in daily conversation (e.g., lakhs, crore) and basic reading of labels, but she does not read or write English fluently.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Rababen is proficient in managing a rural household, including meal planning, preparing traditional Gujarati dishes such as dhokla, thali, and farsan, and maintaining kitchen hygiene. She has practical skills in sewing, mirror work embroidery, and creating simple handicrafts like bamboo baskets and decorative diyas. Her organizational ability helps her keep household accounts, budgeting for festivals, and coordinating community events like village fairs and Navratri gatherings. She also possesses basic knowledge of herbal remedies used in the household and can assist neighbours with modest medical advice. Her curiosity about art has led her to experiment with folk painting motifs (Rangoli designs) and basic sketching.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household management", "Traditional Gujarati cooking", "Sewing and mirror work embroidery", "Bamboo basket weaving", "Basic herbal remedy knowledge", "Community event coordination", "Budgeting and financial planning", "Folk painting (Rangoli designs)", "Basic sketching"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Rababen enjoys listening to Gujarati folk music and participating in Garba during Navratri. She loves experimenting with new recipes and decorating dishes with creative garnishes. In her leisure, she paints simple rangoli patterns, practices basic sketching, and weaves bamboo items. Gardening, especially growing seasonal vegetables for her kitchen, provides her with calm. She also follows local news, reads devotional books in Gujarati, and occasionally watches Hindi films to unwind. Her curiosity leads her to explore online videos about craft techniques whenever she finds time.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Gujarati folk music", "Participating in Garba and Dandiya", "Experimenting with new recipes", "Creating rangoli designs", "Basic sketching", "Bamboo weaving", "Gardening seasonal vegetables", "Reading devotional books", "Watching Hindi films", "Exploring online craft tutorials"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Rababen wishes to deepen her expertise in Gujarati culinary arts and traditional crafts, with the ambition to launch a modest home‑based business selling homemade pickles, farsan, and embroidered items. She aims to enrol in government‑run skill development programs for rural women to learn advanced embroidery and small‑scale food processing. Long‑term, she hopes to become financially independent enough to support her parents, fund her younger siblings' education, and contribute to community development projects. While she values her role as a homemaker, she also aspires to nurture her curiosity about art by attending local workshops and possibly mentoring other village girls.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Rababen Koli is a 28-year-old female from rural Bhavnagar, Gujarat, who is never married and has completed primary education. She is a homemaker with no formal occupation, and her first language is Gujarati. Her lifestyle includes managing household finances and engaging in community duties, with a cultural background rooted in the Koli community.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Radiology / Imaging Report for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Gujarati (code=gu, script=Gujarati). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Gujarat, district=Bhavnagar.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Gujarati script for Gujarati (not English; not a different Indic script).

PLACEMENT: Include [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] as the reporting radiologist (e.g. Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]]) — do not leave the doctor name as untagged prose.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 28
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Gujarat
- district: Bhavnagar
- first_language: Gujarati
- clinical_persona_summary: Rababen Koli is a 28-year-old female from rural Bhavnagar, Gujarat, who is never married and has completed primary education. She is a homemaker with no formal occupation, and her first language is Gujarati. Her lifestyle includes managing household finances and engaging in community duties, with a cultural background rooted in the Koli community.

Document format guide for Radiology / Imaging Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Imaging report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Findings: <radiology prose, untagged>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
Imaging report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Bhavnagar District Hospital]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|Rababen Koli]] [[AGE|28]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0915-001]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0915-001]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Amit Patel]]
[[DISTRICT|Bhavnagar]] | [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] | [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-GJ-015]] | [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
Findings:
Plain radiograph of the chest was performed on [[DATE|15 September 2024]] in the supine position. The lungs are well-inflated with clear costophrenic angles. No acute pulmonary infiltrate, consolidation, or pleural effusion is identified. The cardiac silhouette is within normal limits. The bony structures, including the ribs and thoracic vertebrae, appear unremarkable without any lytic or blastic lesions. No pneumothorax is seen. The visualized upper abdomen shows the liver and spleen to be of normal size and contour. The visualized soft tissues are unremarkable.
Impression:
Normal chest radiograph. No acute cardiopulmonary abnormality.
Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Amit Patel]] on [[DATE|15 September 2024]].

Nursing Notes:
Patient reports a 3-day history of worsening cough, initially dry, now productive of scant blood-streaked sputum. Denies chest pain but reports pleuritic-type discomfort on deep inspiration. Complains of low-grade fever, night sweats, and loss of appetite. States she feels increasingly fatigued over the past month. No known history of TB. No known comorbidities. Last seen at a local health camp 2 months ago for general malaise. Patient is a homemaker from a nearby village. Initial assessment suggests a high likelihood of active pulmonary tuberculosis with possible complications. Blood samples drawn for CBC, ESR, and sputum for AFB smear and culture. Chest X-ray ordered stat. Patient placed on droplet precautions. Oxygen therapy initiated via nasal cannula at 2L/min to maintain SpO2 >92%. IV access established in left forearm. Patient educated on importance of completing full course of treatment and follow-up. Family informed of the provisional diagnosis and plan.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Hindi (code=hi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jharkhand, district=Sahibganj.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Devanagari script for Hindi (not English; not a different Indic script).

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 23
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Juice Clarification Supervisor
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Jharkhand
- district: Sahibganj
- first_language: Hindi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 23-year-old female juice clarification supervisor from rural Jharkhand, never married, with an illiterate education level, speaks Hindi, and resides in Sahibganj district. She works methodically in a production setting, exhibits anxiety, and finds solace in folk music, yoga, and occasional mango consumption. Her lifestyle includes short local trips for shopping and occasional pilgrimages, with a preference for oral communication over written.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton — put demographics + device/network IDs ONLY in a short
session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|...]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
--- chat ---
Patient: <symptom turn — no AGE/GENDER/PHONE re-tags>
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]: <advice turn>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
- URL — URL (Personal Website/Profile) e.g. [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
- IMEI_NUMBER — IMEI Number e.g. [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Sushila Murmu]] [[AGE|23]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240815-001]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|sushila.murmu@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sahibganj District Hospital]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Namaste doctor. I am [[PATIENT_NAME|Sushila Murmu]]. I am 23 years old and this is my first pregnancy. I am feeling very weak and dizzy since the last two days. I also have some pain in my lower abdomen. I am worried.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: Namaste Sushila. I understand your concern. Can you tell me more about the pain? Is it sharp or dull? And how severe is it on a scale of 1 to 10?
Patient: It is a dull pain, not very sharp. The severity is maybe 4 out of 10. It comes and goes. I feel very tired all the time. I have not been able to eat properly.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: Thank you for explaining. Dizziness and fatigue are common in early pregnancy, but we should not ignore them. Have you noticed any bleeding or spotting?
Patient: No, there is no bleeding. Only this pain and dizziness. I am also feeling a bit sick to my stomach in the mornings.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: That is also very common, we call it morning sickness. However, given your symptoms, I want to be sure everything is progressing well. I am arranging an ultrasound for you at the [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sahibganj District Hospital]]. Please come tomorrow morning.
Patient: Okay doctor. What time should I come? And do I need to do anything special before the test?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: Please come at 9 AM. You can have a light meal before coming. Also, please bring your [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]] card if you have it. It will help us access your records faster.
Patient: I do not have an ABHA card. I will just come with my ID. Is it okay?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: Yes, that is perfectly fine. Just bring any photo ID, like your voter ID or Aadhaar card. We will see you tomorrow at 9 AM. In the meantime, try to rest and drink plenty of water.
Patient: Thank you, doctor. I will be there.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: You are welcome, Sushila. Take care.

payload.professional_persona: Gangavva Mathapati is a seasoned vehicle and interior cleaner who combines meticulous dust‑removal techniques with traditional cleaning agents like soda ash and neem leaves, and they pragmatically experiment with simple new tools to boost efficiency while consistently delivering courteous service to clients.
payload.linguistic_persona: Gangavva Mathapati speaks fluent spoken Kannada, skillfully using local idioms and gestures to convey instructions, and they pick up occasional Hindi words from neighbours while understanding basic work‑related English terms like 'vehicle' and 'cleaner'.
payload.sports_persona: Gangavva Mathapati enjoys cheering for the Royal Challengers Bangalore during IPL matches, follows the Indian cricket team's games, and occasionally participates in community badminton sessions at the local Ramanagara club, staying modestly active while preferring low‑impact activities.
payload.arts_persona: Gangavva Mathapati immerses themselves in Kannada folk music, especially songs by K.S. Natha and Hamsalekha, regularly watches classic Dr. Rajkumar films at the Ramanagara community hall, and they enjoy crafting simple beaded decorations inspired by local folk art.
payload.travel_persona: Gangavva Mathapati prefers short, well‑planned trips to nearby destinations such as Nandi Hills, Mysore Palace, and the Tirumala shrine, balancing modest budgets with the desire to experience cultural festivals and family gatherings, and they often travel by shared auto or personal car with a small group of relatives.
payload.culinary_persona: Gangavva Mathapati loves preparing simple traditional dishes such as ragi mudde and bisi bele bath, relishes the earthy flavor of ragi flour, tamarind, and fresh coriander, and they prefer home‑cooked meals over restaurant food, often using seasonal vegetables from their garden.
payload.persona: Gangavva Mathapati is a diligent yet modest cleaning entrepreneur who mixes age‑old neem‑based tricks with occasional gadget cravings, manages money conservatively while secretly splurging on shiny tools, and quietly battles knee arthritis with daily turmeric tea.
payload.cultural_background: Gangavva Mathapati belongs to the Kannada‑speaking community of Ramanagara, a town on the outskirts of Bengaluru known for its granite hills and silk‑cocoon markets. Growing up in an urban environment, she absorbed the everyday rhythms of South‑Indian life – the early‑morning temple bells, the aroma of fresh ragi mudde, and the vibrant celebrations of Ugadi, Dasara and Deepavali. Her family embraces traditional joint‑family values, where respect for elders and a sense of collective duty are paramount. This cultural backdrop shapes her practical outlook, balancing a curiosity for new cleaning tools (jugaad) with a deep reverence for time‑tested household practices.
payload.linguistic_background: Gangavva's linguistic repertoire centres on spoken Kannada. Being illiterate, she cannot read or write Kannada or any other script, but she comfortably engages in daily conversations, understands local idioms, and follows oral instructions at work. She has no formal knowledge of a second language, though occasional Hindi phrases from neighbours seep into her speech. Her exposure to Indian English is limited to work‑related terms such as 'vehicle', 'window' and 'cleaner' that she picks up through listening.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Gangavva is skilled in thorough vehicle interior cleaning, adept at removing dust, stains and odours from seats, carpets and dashboards. She efficiently cleans windows of cars, homes and commercial premises, using a mix of traditional cleaning agents like soda ash, neem leaves and modern supplies where available. Her expertise includes surface disinfection, careful handling of cleaning tools, time management for multiple jobs in a day, and courteous interaction with clients, even though she communicates mainly through gestures and simple spoken Kannada.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Vehicle interior cleaning', 'Window cleaning', 'Surface disinfection', 'Use of traditional cleaning agents', 'Time management', 'Client interaction']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: In her leisure time, Gangavva enjoys listening to Kannada folk songs on the radio, visiting the local temple during festivals, and tending to a small garden of seasonal vegetables and herbs. She loves preparing simple traditional dishes such as ragi mudde and bisi bele bath for family gatherings. Occasionally she engages in hand‑craft activities like beading or making simple decorative items, and she likes watching regional movies at the community hall.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Kannada folk songs', 'Temple visits during festivals', 'Gardening seasonal vegetables and herbs', 'Cooking traditional Kannada dishes', 'Hand‑crafting simple decorative items', 'Watching regional movies']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Gangavva aims to secure a steady flow of cleaning contracts in Ramanagara, hoping to become the trusted go‑to cleaner for local households and small businesses. She wishes to upgrade her tools with a modest motorised vacuum cleaner and to pick up a few efficient cleaning shortcuts (jugaad) from experienced peers. Over the next few years she plans to mentor younger women in her community, helping them earn a livelihood through similar work, while also building a comfortable financial safety net for her family’s future.
payload.occupation: Vehicle, Window and Related Cleaners, Other
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 55-year-old female from Karnataka, Ramanagara, who is currently married, works as a vehicle cleaner, has no formal education, and speaks only Kannada. She lives in an urban area and manages her health conservatively while dealing with knee arthritis. Her lifestyle includes a conservative approach to spending and occasional use of traditional cleaning methods.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER
